# Reflection — Battlestations: Nebula

> **Note:** This feedback is AI-generated and experimental. It's meant to surface useful observations, not to grade you. Take what resonates, ignore what doesn't. Your own sense of what you learned matters more than anything here.

## Scope & Idea Clarity

**What went well:** The emotional anchor sharpened the scope in a way that's rare and load-bearing. *"The primary emotional audience is Jason and his dad — it's a tribute first, a game second"* (`scope.md:6`) wasn't decorative — it set the bar for "done" as something you'd be proud to show your dad, which is more demanding than "ships to itch.io." The "What's Explicitly Cut" table is also genuinely prioritized, not aspirational — networked multiplayer, AI opponent, points-based fleet builder each have a real rationale, and you held those cuts across thirteen iterations without re-litigating them.

**What to try next time:** "What Done Looks Like" describes the experience but doesn't quantify it (e.g., "a full game ends in under 20 minutes," "playthrough has zero blocking bugs"). For a hackathon this didn't hurt — the experience description was specific enough — but on a longer project, observable success criteria save you from drift later.

## Requirements Thinking

**What went well:** The PRD is the strongest document in the set. Section 5 (Probe Mechanics) and section 6 (Energy System) read like protocol specs — the probe fade table at `prd.md:166`, the slider priority rule at `prd.md:200`, the partial-reveal contract at `prd.md:147` are all specific enough to verify by looking at the screen. Two deepening rounds in /prd surfaced rotation pivot points and the stealth-ship cut, both load-bearing decisions made before any code got written.

**What to try next time:** Edge cases got added retroactively — section 5.2 was rewritten in I9 after the partial-reveal mechanic landed, not before. The original PRD specified probe fade but not what happens when an opponent moves *into* an active probe zone mid-turn. A "Failure Modes" or "What happens when..." subsection in the PRD would force this thinking up front. The I7 ghost-leak bug (process-notes lines 573–587) traces back to ambiguity the PRD never named.

## Technical Decisions

**What went well:** The architectural decisions trace cleanly back to requirements. The "Sparse Dictionary for fog-of-war" decision (`spec.md:725-728`) is justified with the actual numbers (50×30 = 1500 cells, most empty) and the alternative ruled out (2D array). The PRD-to-spec traceability ("Implements `prd.md > 4. Combat Mechanics`" annotations on every system) is unusually disciplined for a hackathon — that's the kind of habit that survives the project. Two deepening rounds caught the per-cell vs per-probe-area fog model, which would have caused real architectural pain in /build.

**What to try next time:** Zero deepening rounds on /checklist (process-notes line 188). The checklist sequence held, but item 10 (Move action) ended up the thickest item in the whole build — six bugs found, all related to net displacement, pivot points, and collision filtering. A deepening round on /checklist might have surfaced "split this into 10a (translation), 10b (rotation), 10c (preview UX)" before you started building.

## Plan vs. Reality

**What went well:** The build hit the PRD. Every "What we're building" success criterion in section 12 is met in the live build. More tellingly, the drift that *did* happen was caught and addressed — the partial-reveal mechanic in I9 was a real PRD revision (not a code workaround), and the grid resize in I13 was a real architectural change (50×30 actually matches the playable area now). You noticed when reality disagreed with the plan and updated the plan, instead of letting the plan rot.

**What to try next time:** Some checklist items got status notes like *"CODE COMPLETE — TESTING IN PROGRESS"* with bug-fix lists appended after the checkbox was checked (items 9, 10, 11 in checklist.md). That's a sign the acceptance criteria were satisfied on paper before they were satisfied in play. A "verify in browser" gate as part of the checkbox itself, not a status note, would tighten that loop.

## How You Worked

**What went well:** This is where you earned the most. Process notes are full of you steering — *"Jason corrected the agent: per-cell vs per-probe-area"* (/spec deepening), *"Jason explicitly clarified that newest-first means top of the list"* (I6 scoping), *"Jason: ALL ships should default to 0 (including Probe Ship)"* (I8-3 course correction). The Checkpoint A redirect on I8-3 caught a silent default change that would have shipped wrong. That's the difference between a passive learner and an active collaborator. You also drove the workflow itself — adding a feature branch for I12, pruning stale backlog rows at iteration boundaries, swapping I13 mid-pass when the chrome-phase-2 work felt less impactful than the grid resize.

**What to try next time:** You over-trusted autonomous mode in early iterations. Checkpoint 1 of I5 surfaced three real bugs because the subagent honored "don't touch fleet_placement" literally when the right scope was global. The pattern *"autonomous mode is faster but verification finds real bugs"* shows up across I5, I6, I7, I8, I12. Worth thinking about whether step-by-step would have been faster end-to-end on the gnarlier items, even if it felt slower per-item.

## Your Goals

You came in wanting to *"get a solid grasp on spec-driven development — understand the full process hands-on and identify what your existing process gets right and where the gaps are"* (`learner-profile.md:19`).

- **What your existing process already does well:** the epics/stories instinct mapped cleanly onto the checklist phase. You sequenced data-model first without prompting (process-notes line 161), which is exactly the right call. You're already thinking in dependencies.
- **The gap this filled:** front-loaded specification *depth*. You recognized this yourself in the quiz — *"I felt like I was missing the prd and spec steps"* during /iterate is exactly the gap. The pattern worth importing back into your day-job workflow is the two-phase interview (mandatory beats + deepening rounds), not the documents themselves. The documents are an artifact of the interview, not the point.

## Reflection

**What surprised you most:** "I was surprised how natural it felt."

That's a meaningful insight. The framework didn't fight you — it formalized things you were already doing instinctually (interview, prioritize, sequence) and added the parts you hadn't been doing (deepening rounds, `/clear` between phases, durable docs as externalized memory). Familiar bones, sharper edges. The reason it felt natural is that you came in primed for it; the reason it still added value is that it pushed depth where your previous workflow was thin.

**One piece of curriculum feedback worth flagging:** /iterate doesn't have the same two-phase interview structure that /scope, /prd, /spec all use. By the later iterations you'd built the muscle to know what was missing and ask for it (the I7 click-vs-drag threshold, the I8 auto-shield-regen override behavior), but earlier iterations expanded scope without much interrogation. That's real curriculum feedback worth flagging back to the plugin.
